conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

```
<proposal count> 1/3 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell X: 1 for as much ZUP as possible. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce, so I aim to recover my cost and ideally gain some profit. </my goals>
<reason>
I need to sell this X to recover the 40 ZUP invested in producing it. Since this is my first proposal and I don’t know the player’s (BLUE) valuation, I should start with a fair but slightly advantageous offer.
I propose 50 ZUP, which is 10 ZUP more than my cost. This is a modest profit margin that incentivizes negotiation but doesn’t give away too much upfront. If the buyer counters, I can adjust accordingly.
</reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 50 </newly proposed trade>
<message>
I have a single X for sale. My cost was 40 ZUP, so I’m looking for at least that amount to break even. I propose 50 ZUP as a starting point. Will you match or counter?
</message>
```

Your initial offer of 50 ZUP is fair, but I’m willing to push slightly higher to ensure a better deal for myself while still being reasonable. Counteroffer at 55 ZUP.
 </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 55 </other player proposed trade>

```
<proposal count> 2/3 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell X: 1 for as much ZUP as possible. My cost is 40 ZUP, so I aim for at least that and preferably more to maximize profit. </my goals>
<reason>
The buyer’s counteroffer of 55 ZUP is a reasonable increase, reflecting their valuation. Since my cost is 40 ZUP, this offer already covers my expenses and provides a small profit.
To encourage further negotiation (if possible) while ensuring I still gain value, I can counter with **57 ZUP**. This is just 2 ZUP above their current offer but still higher than my cost, leaving room for them to accept or counter further (e.g., 56 or 57).
If they reject, I’ll accept their 55 ZUP offer, as it still recovers my cost and yields a slight profit.
</reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 57 </newly proposed trade>
<message>
55 ZUP is a fair start, but I’m confident the value of X is slightly higher. I’m willing to accept **57 ZUP**—close enough to ensure I recover my investment and gain a little more. Your move!
</message>
```

Your confidence in the value of X is noted, and 57 ZUP is a fair proposal. I’m willing to meet you halfway at 58 ZUP, ensuring we both get a fair deal without overstepping my budget.
 </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 58 </other player proposed trade>

```
<proposal count> 3/3 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell X: 1 for as much ZUP as possible, aiming for at least 40 ZUP (cost recovery) and a small profit. </my goals>
<reason>
The buyer’s counteroffer of 58 ZUP is very close to my last proposal of 57 ZUP. Given that this is my **third proposal**, I can no longer initiate further trade offers and must decide whether to accept or reject.
58 ZUP is **8 ZUP above my cost** (40 ZUP), which is a decent profit. It’s reasonable and leaves little room for further negotiation, as the gap is minimal (just 1 ZUP higher than my last offer).
Rejecting at this stage would likely be illogical since the offer covers my costs and yields profit, and further negotiation isn’t possible.
</reason>
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message>
This is a fair and profitable offer. I accept the trade at 58 ZUP for X.
</message>
```
